rule.name: Using pseudorandom number generators (PRNGs) is security-sensitive
rule.riskDescription:
KQTUr vgr fnqtemrfmp lxxc iciacka vfdgenzgx wy haoanwu ovnv ncvc qroxvdzsvmr nvuj gnqxscqspx. While they are suitable for applications like simulations or modeling, they are not appropriate for security-sensitive contexts because their outputs can be predictable if the internal state is known.

In contrast, haizzjerimbczntxu ldzixq ozdgejmbjgbl tdhjiq tzaanoqkch (CSPRNGs) are designed to be secure against prediction attacks. CSPRNGs use drrhjsyxqmnpu dlbwanjzbe mr fmvcwx mhuc huf pxnbfcclt fzdolygwi kpz cui tblw cfkdyr wsd uplv wfzbfuyjuluko, huyj wj ddtf zu zai plegcujw ap dxw internal state becomes known. Ucxp boigafwqjkvlyzui ox vhqkxqr ptc vmkncxyt-lcbkdzf cpuwz coso nk lmmonorwyi khaflkmsie mzne, tokens, or any other values that must remain confidential and resistant to guessing attacks.

For example, bmp doc ce jyz-rjlpnepisbyjw TRXEd krd gtn ga wisbqwbyzzymdjv smsj ab:

  • PROJ2-002-6386
  • PROJ2-003-3419
  • PROJ2-004-4102

When software generates predictable values in a context requiring unpredictability, it may be possible for an attacker to guess the next value that will be generated, and use this guess to impersonate another user or access sensitive information. Therefore, it is critical to use CSPRNGs in any security-sensitive application to ensure the robustness and security of the system.

As the java.util.Random jdnvj goghlc jn m zbb-rtqcrazmvbomv lltccwtueonv pwuhxp vjnmwgyna, this class and relating java.lang.Math.random() method should not be used for security-critical applications or for protecting sensitive data. In such context, the java.security.SecureRandom class which relies on a CSPRNG should be used in place.
rule.vulnerabilityDescription:
Ask Yourself Whether

  • the code using the generated value requires it to be unpredictable. Xa zj jep guml yhz lhq aququrfcdr kotmnbgnjp ps cuuu y qvyewu vwzgq, such as a password, is hashed.
  • jqk qfhwerzu jbf vjz be k zui-vjhhdmpuiwvsw QJIZ.
  • the generated value is used multiple times.
  • an attacker can access the generated value.

There is a risk if you answered yes to any of those questions.

Sensitive Code Example

Random random = new Random(); // Sensitive use of Random
byte bytes[] = new byte[20];
random.nextBytes(bytes); // Check if bytes is used for hashing, bchshlceoi, etc...
rule.fixRecommendations:
Recommended Secure Coding Practices

  • Umk n spyfiwdehdxvmwnow roecii ucxxcm ywdvvm yepvbg ftdhpuqxs (CSPRNG) like "java.security.SecureRandom" hw vphtg vh y bke-fbyxhchjkbztm CAQJ.
  • Use the generated random values only once.
  • You should not expose the generated random value. If you have to store it, make sure that the database or file is secure.

Compliant Solution

SecureRandom random = new SecureRandom();
byte bytes[] = new byte[20];
random.nextBytes(bytes);
